items.front: What does doping do to a pure (intrinsic) semiconductor?
items.back: Doping adds impurity atoms to set the dominant carrier type. Donors (e.g. phosphorus, group V) create n-type material with excess free electrons; acceptors (e.g. boron, group III) create p-type material with excess holes. This raises conductivity far above intrinsic silicon and is controllable over many orders of magnitude.
items.hint: Group V vs group III impurities.

items.front: What is the depletion region of a PN junction?
items.back: At the metallurgical junction, electrons and holes diffuse across and recombine, leaving behind ionized dopant atoms. This forms a region depleted of free carriers with a built-in electric field (and built-in potential ~0.6-0.7V for Si). The field opposes further diffusion and sets up the junction's barrier.
items.hint: Carriers diffuse, leaving fixed charge behind.

items.front: Contrast forward bias and reverse bias of a diode.
items.back: Forward bias (anode positive w.r.t. cathode) shrinks the depletion region and lowers the barrier, allowing large current once V exceeds the turn-on knee. Reverse bias (cathode positive) widens the depletion region, blocks conduction, and passes only a tiny saturation/leakage current Is until breakdown.
items.hint: Which polarity narrows the depletion region?

items.front: State the Shockley diode equation and define its terms.
items.back: I = Is*(e^(V/(n*Vt)) - 1). Is is the reverse saturation current, V the voltage across the junction, n the ideality factor (~1-2), and Vt the thermal voltage = kT/q (~25.85 mV at 300 K, often rounded to ~26 mV). The exponential dominates in forward bias; in strong reverse bias I approaches -Is.
items.hint: Exponential of V over n times Vt, minus one.

items.prompt: Approximately what is the thermal voltage Vt = kT/q at room temperature (~300 K)?
items.explanation: Vt = kT/q is about 25.85 mV near 300 K, conventionally rounded to ~26 mV (or 25 mV). It is not the 0.7V Si forward drop, which is a different quantity arising from the exponential I-V relation.

items.prompt: Typical forward voltage drops: which set is correct for Si, Ge, and Schottky diodes?
items.explanation: Silicon PN junctions conduct at ~0.7V, germanium at ~0.3V, and Schottky (metal-semiconductor) at ~0.2-0.4V. The lower Schottky drop and fast switching make it attractive for rectifiers and detectors.

items.prompt: In strong reverse bias (and below breakdown), the Shockley equation predicts the current approaches:
items.explanation: For V negative and |V| >> n*Vt, e^(V/(n*Vt)) -> 0, so I -> Is*(0 - 1) = -Is. The current saturates at a small reverse leakage of magnitude Is until reverse breakdown occurs.

items.front: Why does the silicon diode forward drop have a negative temperature coefficient (~ -2 mV/C)?
items.back: Is rises strongly with temperature (roughly doubling every ~10C), so for a fixed forward current the required V falls by about 2 mV per degree C. This is exploited in temperature sensors and bandgap references, and matters for biasing because a diode/transistor junction conducts more at the same V as it heats.
items.hint: Is grows with T, so V at fixed I must fall.

items.front: What is the small-signal dynamic resistance r_d of a forward-biased diode?
items.back: r_d = n*Vt / I, the slope inverse of the I-V curve at the operating point. At room temperature with n=1, r_d ~ 26 mV / I (e.g. ~26 ohm at 1 mA, ~2.6 ohm at 10 mA). It models the diode for small AC signals riding on a DC bias, as in detectors and mixers.
items.hint: Differentiate the diode equation; r_d = dV/dI.

items.front: List the common piecewise diode models from simplest to most detailed.
items.back: 1) Ideal: short when forward, open when reverse (0V drop). 2) Constant-drop: fixed ~0.7V when conducting, open otherwise. 3) Piecewise-linear: constant drop plus a series resistance r_s for the I-V slope. 4) Small-signal: the operating-point model using r_d = n*Vt/I for AC analysis.
items.hint: Ideal -> constant drop -> add series R -> AC r_d.

items.prompt: For a half-wave rectifier driven by a sine of peak Vp (using the constant-drop model with one Si diode), the peak output across the load is about:
items.explanation: A half-wave rectifier conducts through one diode on positive half-cycles, so the peak output is Vp minus one diode drop (~0.7V). The bridge subtracts two drops (~1.4V) because two diodes conduct in series.

items.prompt: A full-wave BRIDGE rectifier compared with a full-wave CENTER-TAPPED rectifier:
choices: Uses 1 diode vs 2 and needs no transformer
Uses 4 diodes with two drops per cycle but no center tap; center-tapped uses 2 diodes (one drop) but needs a tapped secondary
Cannot be filtered with a capacitor
items.explanation: The bridge uses 4 diodes (two conduct in series each half-cycle, ~1.4V total drop) and a simple secondary. The center-tapped version uses 2 diodes (one drop ~0.7V) but requires a center-tapped winding and each diode sees higher PIV (~2*Vp).

items.front: What is Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) and roughly what value must the diodes survive in (a) a bridge and (b) a center-tapped full-wave rectifier?
items.back: PIV is the maximum reverse voltage a diode must block. In a bridge, each diode sees about Vp (one secondary peak, minus a drop). In a center-tapped rectifier, the off diode sees the full winding peak-to-peak, roughly 2*Vp. With a filter cap charged near Vp, derate generously above these figures.
items.hint: Center-tapped stresses the off diode with ~2x the bridge.

items.front: Capacitor-input filter: give the ripple formula and the cap-sizing rearrangement.
items.back: Approximate ripple Vr ~ I_load / (f * C), where f is the ripple frequency (line freq for half-wave, 2*line for full-wave). Rearranged to size the cap: C ~ I_load / (f * Vr). Larger C and higher f reduce ripple; full-wave halves the discharge time vs half-wave, so it needs about half the capacitance for the same ripple.
items.hint: Q = C*V and I = C*dV/dt over the discharge interval.

items.prompt: A full-wave bridge on a 60 Hz line drives 0.5 A and you want <= 1V peak-to-peak ripple. Using Vr ~ I/(f*C) with f=120 Hz, the filter cap is about:
items.explanation: C ~ I/(f*Vr) = 0.5 / (120 * 1) = 0.00417 F = ~4170 uF. Note f=120 Hz because a full-wave rectifier produces two output pulses per line cycle. A half-wave version (f=60 Hz) would need roughly double.

items.prompt: Why does a larger filter capacitor cause higher peak diode/transformer currents even though average load current is unchanged?
choices: It does not; current is constant
The cap recharges only during a brief interval near the peak, so charge must be delivered in short high-amplitude current pulses
Larger caps lower the line frequency
It raises the diode forward voltage
items.explanation: With a stiff cap the output stays near Vp and the diodes conduct only in a narrow window near each peak. All the charge delivered to the load over the cycle must be replenished in that short time, producing tall, narrow current spikes that stress the diodes and transformer.

items.front: In a power-supply front end, why are Schottky diodes often chosen for the output rectifier of a switching converter?
items.back: Schottky diodes have a low forward drop (~0.3V) that cuts conduction loss, and they are majority-carrier devices with essentially no reverse-recovery charge, so they switch cleanly at high frequency. Tradeoffs: higher reverse leakage, lower reverse voltage ratings, and worse leakage at high temperature versus silicon PN diodes.
items.hint: Low Vf and fast switching vs limited reverse rating.

items.prompt: The piecewise-linear model adds what to the constant-drop model to better fit the measured forward I-V curve?
choices: A series resistance r_s representing the curve's finite slope
items.explanation: Piecewise-linear approximates conduction as a fixed offset (~0.7V) in series with a resistance r_s, giving a sloped line instead of a vertical one. This captures the additional drop at high current better than the flat constant-drop model.

items.front: What happens at reverse breakdown, and how does a Zener diode use it?
items.back: Beyond the reverse breakdown voltage, current rises sharply (via Zener tunneling below ~5V and avalanche above ~6V) while the voltage stays nearly constant. A Zener diode is designed to operate in this region as a voltage reference or shunt regulator, holding Vz across itself as long as it is kept within its current limits.
items.hint: Sharp knee where V is nearly fixed.

items.front: Sketch the basic Zener shunt regulator and its design current relation.
items.back: A series resistor Rs feeds a node where the Zener (cathode to +) shunts to ground and the load taps across it. I_Rs = (Vin - Vz)/Rs splits into I_zener + I_load. Rs must be chosen so the Zener stays above its minimum current at max load / min Vin, and below its power limit at min load / max Vin.
items.hint: Series R sets total current; Zener absorbs the slack.

items.prompt: A Zener shunt regulator stops regulating (output sags below Vz) when:
choices: Load current rises so high that little or no current is left for the Zener, dropping it out of breakdown
Vin exceeds Vz by too much
items.explanation: If I_load approaches (Vin - Vz)/Rs, the Zener current falls below its minimum and it leaves the breakdown region, so the output drops with load. The other classic failure is the opposite extreme: at light load and high Vin the Zener can exceed its power rating and overheat.

items.prompt: Which is a fundamental limitation of a simple Zener shunt regulator versus a series-pass linear regulator?
choices: It cannot produce a stable voltage
Poor efficiency and limited load range, since Rs and the Zener must waste current; load and line regulation are set by Zener impedance Rz
items.explanation: The shunt regulator continuously dissipates current in Rs and the Zener regardless of load, so it is inefficient and tolerates only a limited load range. Output stiffness is limited by the Zener's dynamic impedance Rz; precision designs buffer it or use a dedicated reference IC.

items.front: What is a diode clipper (limiter), and give a protection use case.
items.back: A clipper limits a signal's excursion by conducting once it exceeds a threshold, clamping the peak to about that level. Two diodes to the rails (or back-to-back at an input) protect an ADC, op-amp, or logic input by shunting overvoltage transients. This is the diode-clamp input protection familiar from instrumentation and radar receiver front-ends.
items.hint: Diode conducts and holds the peak at a set level.

items.prompt: A diode CLAMP (DC restorer) differs from a CLIPPER in that it:
choices: Shifts the entire waveform's DC level so a chosen point sits at a reference, without changing its shape
items.explanation: A clamp uses a series capacitor plus a diode (and optional bias) to add a DC offset so the waveform's peak (or valley) rests at a reference level, preserving the AC shape. A clipper instead chops off part of the waveform that exceeds a threshold.

items.front: How does a half-wave (peak) voltage doubler work?
items.back: A clamp stage (series cap + diode) shifts the input up by ~Vp, then a peak-rectifier stage (diode + cap) captures the ~2*Vp peak. Cascading clamp/peak stages yields triplers/quadruplers (Cockcroft-Walton multiplier). Useful for generating high DC voltages from a modest AC source, at the cost of poor regulation and high output impedance.
items.hint: Clamp then peak-detect; stack stages for higher multiples.

items.prompt: Why are back-to-back diodes (or a TVS/clamp) placed at a sensitive analog input rather than relying on the device's internal protection?
choices: To shunt ESD and overvoltage transients away before they reach the input, holding the pin within a safe range
items.explanation: External clamp diodes (to the rails or a TVS) divert transient energy and limit the pin voltage to roughly a diode drop beyond the rails, protecting the input stage. Internal clamps exist but have limited current handling, so external protection is added for robustness in harsh environments.

items.front: Why must a Zener (or clamp) diode's dynamic impedance Rz be considered, not just its nominal Vz?
items.back: Rz is the slope of the breakdown region; the regulated voltage actually moves by delta_I * Rz as current changes, degrading load and line regulation. It also injects noise. Lower-impedance Zeners regulate better, and references near 5-6V have the lowest temperature coefficient because Zener and avalanche tempcos cancel.
items.hint: V across it is not perfectly flat; slope = Rz.

items.front: What is a varactor (varicap) diode and what is it used for?
items.back: A varactor is a reverse-biased diode used as a voltage-controlled capacitor: the depletion width (and thus junction capacitance) varies with reverse voltage. It tunes VCOs, PLLs, RF filters, and electronic tuning circuits, replacing mechanical tuning. Hyperabrupt-junction types give a wider, more linear C-vs-V tuning range.
items.hint: Reverse-bias varies depletion capacitance for tuning.

items.prompt: A varactor diode is operated in which bias condition?
items.explanation: A varactor is used reverse-biased (below breakdown), where increasing reverse voltage widens the depletion region and reduces junction capacitance. This C-vs-V relationship provides the voltage-controlled tuning element for VCOs and tuned circuits.

items.front: How does a photodiode operate, and in what two modes?
items.back: Light absorbed in or near the junction generates electron-hole pairs that produce a current. Photovoltaic mode (zero bias) gives low noise and is used in solar cells / precision light sensing; photoconductive mode (reverse bias) widens the depletion region for faster response and higher bandwidth, used in optical receivers. A PIN photodiode adds an intrinsic layer for speed and efficiency.

items.prompt: What primarily distinguishes a PIN diode from an ordinary PN diode in RF use?
choices: An intrinsic (undoped) layer between P and N makes it act as a current-controlled RF resistor / switch and attenuator at high frequency
items.explanation: The wide intrinsic region stores charge so that at RF the PIN diode behaves as a variable resistance set by its DC forward (bias) current, enabling RF switches, attenuators, and phase shifters. It does not rectify the RF the way a small junction would.

items.front: Why is a Schottky diode the preferred detector/mixer element in RF receivers and radar?
items.back: As a majority-carrier device it has negligible minority-carrier storage and very fast response, plus a low turn-on (~0.2-0.4V) giving good detection sensitivity. Low junction capacitance and low series resistance let it work at microwave frequencies as an envelope detector, mixer, or power detector.
items.hint: No stored charge, low Vf, fast at microwave.

items.prompt: A tunnel (Esaki) diode is notable in microwave circuits because it exhibits:
choices: A negative-resistance region in its forward I-V curve, usable for oscillators and amplifiers
items.explanation: Heavy doping makes a tunnel diode conduct by quantum tunneling at low forward voltage, then current DECREASES with increasing voltage over a range, giving negative differential resistance. That property allows high-frequency oscillators and amplifiers.

items.prompt: A Gunn diode generates microwave power based on:
choices: A PN junction in forward bias
The transferred-electron (negative differential mobility) effect in bulk n-GaAs, not a junction at all
items.explanation: A Gunn diode is not a junction diode; it relies on the transferred-electron effect in bulk III-V material (e.g. GaAs), where electrons move to a lower-mobility valley, producing negative differential resistance and microwave oscillation in a resonant cavity.

items.front: What determines an LED's forward voltage, and why does it differ from a silicon rectifier?
items.back: An LED's forward drop is set by the semiconductor bandgap of its emitted color: ~1.8-2.2V for red, up to ~3-3.5V for blue/white, versus ~0.7V for a silicon rectifier. Recombination across the wider bandgap emits a photon, so higher-energy (bluer) light requires a higher forward voltage. LEDs are driven with a current-limiting resistor or current source.
items.hint: Drop tracks the bandgap / photon energy (color).

items.prompt: When choosing between a Schottky and a silicon PN diode for a given role, the main Schottky drawback to weigh against its low drop and speed is:
choices: Higher reverse leakage and lower reverse-voltage ratings (worsening at high temperature)
items.explanation: Schottky diodes trade their low forward drop and fast switching for higher reverse leakage and generally lower reverse breakdown voltage, both of which worsen with temperature. Where high reverse voltage blocking or very low leakage is needed, a silicon PN diode is preferred.
